description: The Taiwan’s carbon fee is a national carbon pricing mechanism established under the Climate Change Response Act to put a price on greenhouse gas emissions and support Taiwan’s net-zero transition. Launched on January 1, 2025, the program initially applies to large emitters in the power, gas supply, and manufacturing sectors, with fees charged per tonne of CO₂ equivalent emissions. A standard fee rate of NT$300/tCO₂e applies, alongside preferential rates of NT$50 or NT$100/tCO₂e for entities that submit approved emissions-reduction plans, aiming to incentivize early action while gradually strengthening Taiwan’s climate policy framework.
recent_developments: New draft guidelines has been released by the Ministry of Environment in December 2025 on fee reductions for certain “high carbon leakage risk” industries with the goal of enhancing the carbon fee system to align with the EU’s carbon border adjustment mechanism (CBAM). Companies that get their self-determined reduction plans approved can receive significant fee discounts — in some cases up to 80 % off through a combination of preferential rate and leakage risk coefficient adjustments.
coverage_notes: Not applicable
relation_notes: Not applicable

name: Taiwan, China ETS
description:
Taiwan, China enacted the Climate Change Response Act in 2023, amending the 2015 GHG Reduction and Management Act, to legislate a net-zero emissions target by 2050.

Covered entities are required to calculate and pay their carbon fees based on 2025 GHG emissions by May 2026.

Mandatory emissions reporting for entities with annual emissions above 25,000 tCO₂e in the energy and industry sectors has been in place since 2014. The Climate Change Response Act also provides for domestic early action and offset credits, and the recognition of international offset credits under standards to be determined.
recent_developments:
The Ministry of Environment announced in October 2024 the intention to transfer Taiwan's "carbon fee" into an ETS within four years.

In January 2025, the Vice Premier stated that the government aims to accelerate this transition, with a pilot ETS potentially launching in the second half of 2026 and full implementation anticipated in 2027 or 2028, operating in parallel with the carbon fee scheme. MOENV is also conducting consultations on a Taiwan Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM).

name: Tamaulipas carbon tax
share_of_emissions_covered: 59% of jurisdiction emissions, 0.0433% of global emissions

description:
In July 2020, the Mexican state of Tamaulipas passed legislation enacting a carbon tax (Derecho de Emisión de Gases a la Atmósfera). In 2022, the carbon tax was repealed and in December 2023 an updated proposal for a carbon tax was presented. The tax was relaunched in 2024, and updated in dec 2025 through the Ley de Hacienda for 2026.

The carbon tax applied to fixed sources and facilities that emit more than 25,000 metric tCO2e of GHG. Revenues will be used to finance policies and programs for climate change mitigation.

The carbon tax rate was set at three times the daily "Unit of Measurement and Update" [Unidad de Medida y Actualización (UMA)], an economic reference for determining taxation and other payments set in legislation.
recent_developments: The 2025 reform to the Tamaulipas CT Law eliminates the requirement to validate carbon offsets through a Low-Carbon Label (LCL) to access tax incentives. While this reduces administrative burdens, it creates a regulatory gap by removing formal certification mechanisms for offset quality and origin. The absence of clear authorities, approved methodologies, and technical guidelines generates legal and financial uncertainty for companies. To ensure transparency, scientific rigor, and consistency with national emissions inventories, administrative simplification must be accompanied by well-defined eligibility rules for carbon compensation.
coverage_notes:
The tax must be paid by all entities and companies in the state with fixed sources that directly emit GHG emissions (CO2, CH4, N2O, HFCs, PFCs, SF6). 
The tax applies to fixed sources with activities that generate more than 25,000 tons of CO2eq per year into the atmosphere taxed in the territory of the State. 
Emissions must be directly measured or estimated. Entities may use the federal and the state Registry on Emissions and Pollution Transfers (Registro de Emisiones y Transferencias de Contaminantes, RETC) for this purpose.
relation_notes: Not applicable

name: Thailand carbon tax
recent_developments: The Cabinet greenlit the draft Climate Change Bill which creates a national policy body and legal framework for a national carbon tax and ETS.
coverage_notes: Carbon taxation will apply to products with high greenhouse gas emissions during use, notably fossil-fuel-based products, as specified in the annex to the draft Act. The emissions trading scheme (ETS) will apply to industrial processes, covering Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions. DCCE will study how the ETS can be used in conjunction with carbon taxation to reduce regulatory burdens and avoid overlap.

name: Thailand ETS
description:
Thailand is developing a legislative framework for carbon pricing for several years. Building on the 2018 National Reform Plan and more than a decade of voluntary market infrastructure work by the Thailand Greenhouse Gas Management Organization (TGO), the Cabinet approved the Draft Climate Change Act and forwarded it to the Office of the Council of State for legal review prior to parliamentary consideration in December 2025, with enforcement anticipated in 2027.

The approved Draft Act establishes an ETS, a carbon tax, a carbon border adjustment mechanism modelled on the EU's CBAM, and a regulated market for carbon credits. It also creates a Climate Fund financed in part through ETS and CBAM revenues. Primary supervisory authority is assigned to the Department of Climate Change and Environment (DCCE), which will be charged with developing up to 50 subsidiary regulations for the ETS, carbon credit markets, and the MRV system.

The ETS will operate within industry-specific GHG ceilings set by the DCCE under the National Committee on Climate Change (NCCC). The sectors to be covered will be identified in subsidiary law following a data collection period planned for 2027 and 2028, with a pilot ETS potentially launching in 2029. Allocation plans will be updated every three to five years and will define covered activities, annual caps, offset credit limits, and the auctioned share of allowances for each sector. The DCCE will establish a registry and authorize an allowance trading center; in January 2025, the Securities and Exchange Commission announced plans for a carbon credit trading platform operated by the Stock Exchange of Thailand.
recent_developments:
In December 2025, the Thai Cabinet approved the Draft Climate Change Act, establishing an ETS, carbon tax, carbon border adjustment mechanism, and regulated carbon credit market. The bill was forwarded to the Office of the Council of State for legal review before submission to Parliament, with enforcement anticipated in 2027. 

In January 2025, the Securities and Exchange Commission announced a new carbon credit trading platform to be operated by the Stock Exchange of Thailand. In February 2025, the Stock Exchange signed a memorandum of understanding with the Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) to leverage financial technology and market expertise for the platform.

name: Tianjin pilot ETS
share_of_emissions_covered: 18% of jurisdiction emissions, 0.0662% of global emissions

description: Tianjin launched its pilot ETS in 2013. The Tianjin pilot ETS is a cap-and-trade system. Covered entities must surrender one allowance per tCO2e emitted for all their covered emissions. The system covers petrochemicals, chemicals, oil and gas exploration, papermaking, aviation and building materials. The Tianjin pilot ETS operates in parallel with the China national ETS. Power sector entities transferred to the China national ETS in 2019; steel and cement entities transferred in 2024.
recent_developments:
In February 2025, the Tianjin Ecology and Environment Bureau (EEB) published the Work Plan for Expanding the Scope of Coverage of the Tianjin Carbon Emissions Trading Market, which expanded coverage to ports and maritime transport from compliance year 2025. 

In June 2025, the Tianjin Municipal People's Government updated the Interim Measures for Management of Emissions Trading in Tianjin to align with the China national ETS and improve MRV and market oversight. 

In October 2025, the Tianjin Pilot ETS completed compliance for year 2024 with a reported 100% compliance rate, the tenth consecutive year of full compliance. 

The Tianjin EEB, the Development and Reform Commission, and the Tianjin branch of the People's Bank of China jointly issued an Action Plan for Deepening Reform of the Tianjin ETS in October 2025, setting out development priorities to 2030 including lowering inclusion thresholds, expanding sector coverage and non-CO₂ GHGs, developing output-based benchmarks, increasing the auctioned share of allowances, and cancelling free allowances for entities transferred to the China national ETS. 

In December 2025, the EEB published the 2025 allocation plan confirming 40 MtCO2 in total allowances.
coverage_notes:
The inclusion threshold was 20,000 tCO2 per year from 2013 to 2024 and was lowered to 10,000 tCO2 per year from the 2025 compliance year, considering both direct and indirect emissions. 

Covered sectors include, petrochemicals, chemicals, oil and gas exploration, papermaking, airport, building materials, non-ferrous metals, pharmaceutical manufacturing, machinery and equipment manufacturing, agricultural and food processing, food and beverage, salt-mining, rubber and plastic products, electronic equipment manufacturing, and from compliance year 2025, ports and maritime transport. 

 Steel and cement entities transferred to the China national ETS in 2024; power sector entities transferred in 2019.
relation_notes: Coverage of power sector emissions was transferred to the the Chinese national ETS.

name: Tokyo CaT
share_of_emissions_covered: 20% of jurisdiction emissions, 0.0229% of global emissions

description:
The Tokyo Cap-and-Trade Program is a baseline-and-credit system. Launched in April 2010, the Tokyo CaT is Japan's first mandatory carbon market and is linked to the Saitama ETS. The Tokyo CaT introduced mandatory emission reduction targets for large emitters.

Each covered facility has its own cap, which serves as the “baseline” from which it must achieve its reduction target. Facilities’ baselines are calculated using base-year emissions and a compliance factor. Compliance factors are determined based on the type of facility and factors such as expected energy efficiency gains and the extent to which they consume energy supplied by other facilities.
recent_developments:
Tokyo Metropolitan Government's Cap-and-Trade Program's fourth compliance period (FY2025 to FY2029) started in April 2025. The compliance factor rose to 50% for office buildings and 48% for factories. Off-site renewable energy, including self-consignment and power purchase agreements, is treated as zero-emissions, and renewable energy certificates may be deducted from energy-related CO₂ emissions. Actual emission factors, rather than fixed factors, now apply to electricity, heat, and city gas supplied by retailers based on facility contracts. 

From April 2025, excess emission reduction credits are awarded only for reductions from energy efficiency and renewable energy measures and credits are no longer issued for certification improvements or emission factor adjustments. In March 2025, the TMG published results for FY2023 showing covered facility emissions totalled 11.32 MtCO2 which shows a 31% reduction below base-year emissions.
coverage_notes: The Tokyo CaT applies to energy-use related CO2 emissions from the industry and buildings sectors.
relation_notes: The Tokyo CaT is linked to the Saitama ETS and operators can use credits from the Saitama ETS to meet their compliance obligations.

name: Türkiye ETS
description:
Türkiye has been preparing for the introduction of a national ETS to support its mitigation targets, with the legal framework taking shape in 2025. In July 2025, Türkiye enacted its first Climate Law, establishing the TR ETS and creating the institutional structure to administer it. The Carbon Market Board, chaired by the Minister of Environment, Urbanization and Climate Change, is responsible for approving national allocation plans, setting free allocation volumes, and determining offset credit limits. 

The Climate Law designates the Department of Climate Change as the competent authority for MRV, which has operated at installation level for energy and industry since 2015. Over 800 facilities are covered under the MRV regulation, encompassing combustion installations with rated thermal input of 20 MW or above. Collectively, these facilities account for around half of Turkey's aggregate GHG emissions.

The ETS aligns with Türkiye's "2053 Long-Term Climate Strategy" and the "Medium-Term Program 2026 to 2028", both of which anticipate ETS implementation consistent with EU CBAM requirements. The Climate Law earmarks up to 10% of ETS revenues for just transition measures benefiting vulnerable groups and sectors.
recent_developments: In July 2025, Climate Law entered into force, providing the statutory basis for the ETS and establishing the Carbon Market Board and related institutional arrangements. A draft ETS regulation was published in July 2025 for stakeholder consultation, addressing implementation topics including allocation, MRV, and registry operation. The pilot phase of the ETS is expected to start in 2026. In November 2025, Turkiye submitted its updated NDC (NDC 3.0), pledging to limit national emissions to 643 MtCO2e in 2035, a reduction of 466 MtCO2e compared to the business-as-usual scenario. Turkiye will host COP 31 in 2026.

name: Ukraine ETS
description:
Ukraine plans to establish a national ETS in line with its obligations under the “Ukraine-EU Association Agreement”, which entered into force in September 2017. Issues related to climate change are addressed in Article 365 (c) Title V and in an annex to the agreement, which outlines steps for the implementation of a national ETS 

The country has since established a national MRV system, with its scope partially covering activities similar to EU ETS activities, to provide a solid basis for the upcoming ETS. Since 2021, the MRV procedures as adopted in the framework law on MRV have been applied by regulated installations. 

In October 2024, the Parliament approved the "Law on Basic Principles of State Climate Policy", mandating the establishment of an ETS with an absolute cap on covered-sector emissions. Following stakeholder consultation in 2024, a governmental decree approving the "Action Plan for the Establishment of a National GHG Trading System" was adopted in February 2025. The action plan sets the first operational phase of the ETS to commence from 2028, with the launch of a second operational phase starting no earlier than three years after martial law is halted or cancelled.
recent_developments:
In October 2024, the Ukrainian Parliament approved the "Law on Basic Principles of State Climate Policy", establishing the mandate to create a national ETS with an absolute cap. In January 2025, mandatory reporting under the MRV system was reinstated. 

In February 2025, the government adopted an action plan for the national GHG ETS, setting 2028 as the start of the first operational phase and aligning key design parameters with the EU ETS Directive.

share_of_emissions_covered: 10% of jurisdiction emissions, 0.0727% of global emissions
sectors_covered: Agriculture, forestry and fishing fuel use
fuels_covered: CO2
description: The Carbon Price Support (CPS) is a rate is a
recent_developments: Great Britain tax on fossil fuels used in electricity generation in Great Britain. The CPS, together with the UK ETS price, has contributed to a rapid decline in power sector emissions. For example, coal has been driven out of the UK’s power generation – electricity generated by coal fell from 40% in 2012 just before CPS was introduced to just 1% in 2023, and the last coal fired power station closed in late 2024.
coverage_notes: The Carbon Price Support applies to all fossil fuels (petroleum gas or other gaseous hydrocarbon in a liquid state; coal and other solid fossil fuels) used to generate electricity in Great Britain, with some exceptions.
relation_notes:
CPS applies to electricity generators in Great Britain, alongside their liability under the UK ETS. The two schemes together form the total carbon price for electricity generators in Great Britain.

Electricity generators in Northern Irelands participate in the EU Emissions Trading Scheme only. 

Following the announcement at Budget 2025 that indirect emissions will not be in scope of the UK CBAM in 2027, CPS impacts on domestic producers will not be accounted for in the calculation of the CBAM rate for imported goods.

Following the EU’s latest CBAM publication in December 2025, the EU have confirmed that CPS will be considered as part of the UK’s effective domestic carbon price (ETS +CPS). At current prices, this would mean the EU CBAM liability on electricity produced in Great Britain and exported to the EU should not face a charge. ETS linking would mean that both the UK and EU ETS prices are the same, meaning CPS would no longer serve this purpose as effective carbon prices would be equal purely on ETS terms.

name: UK ETS
share_of_emissions_covered: 25% of jurisdiction emissions, 0.1817% of global emissions
sectors_covered: Agriculture, forestry and fishing fuel use
fuels_covered: Diesel; Other oil products; Gasoline; Kerosene; LPG; Natural gas; Jet fuel; Coal
price_or_market_management: Auction with floor (GBP22), cost containment mechanism
description:
The UK Emissions Trading Scheme (UK ETS) began operating in January 2021, following the departure of the UK (excluding power operators located in Northern Ireland) from the EU ETS. Verified emissions from stationary UK ETS operators currently cover around a quarter of the UK’s territorial GHG emissions. The first phase of the UK ETS runs until 2030. 
 
The UK ETS covers around 1,000 installations in the power and industrial sectors, as well as around 400 aircraft operators. Aviation activity covered includes flights within the UK as well as flights departing the UK to the European Economic Area (EEA) and Switzerland. Domestic maritime emissions will be brought under the scheme from July 1, 2026.

Covered entities must surrender allowances for all their in-scope emissions. Allowances are allocated primarily through auctioning, with a portion freely allocated based on product benchmarks to mitigate the risk of carbon leakage. The system operates a cost containment mechanism (CCM) and an auction reserve price (ARP) to support market stability.
 
The UK ETS cap trajectory is consistent with the UK's target of achieving net zero by 2050. Expansion to waste incineration and energy-from-waste (full inclusion from 2028, preceded by a mandatory MRV-only period from January 2026), domestic maritime (from July 2026), and GHG removals (from 2029) is confirmed.

A UK Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) applying to emissions-intensive imports from the aluminum, cement, fertilizers, hydrogen, iron, and steel sectors will enter into force on January 1, 2027. 

In May 2025, the UK and EU governments announced their intention to work towards linking their respective systems.
recent_developments:
In 2025, the UK ETS Authority focused on preparing scope expansions, refining the free allocation framework, and laying the groundwork for post-2030 operation. In February 2025, the Authority consulted on extending the UK ETS cap beyond 2030.

In May 2025, the UK and EU agreed to work towards linking their respective systems, with the envisaged arrangement enabling mutual recognition of allowances for compliance, cross-system trading, and mutual CBAM exemptions. 

In July 2025, the Authority published interim responses to the 2024 consultations on scope expansion and greenhouse gas removals, confirming domestic maritime coverage from July 2026 and a mandatory MRV-only period for waste incineration and energy-from-waste from January 2026 ahead of full inclusion from 2028. Integration of UK-based engineered removals was confirmed from 2029, subject to legislation.

In October 2025, the Authority launched a consultation on the potential impact of removing aviation free allocation on regional air connectivity. 

In November 2025, the Authority published its final maritime consultation response, confirming the expansion to domestic maritime including cap adjustments, exemptions, and a delay for offshore vessels until January 2027. The Authority also launched a separate consultation on including international maritime voyages from 2028. In November 2025, the Authority's final Free Allocation Review response confirmed allocation rules for the 2027–2030 period, maintaining carbon leakage protection for most at-risk sectors while phasing out free allocation for CBAM-covered sectors from 2027.

In December 2025, the Authority announced the Phase 2 extension of the UK ETS from 2031 to 2040 and confirmed decisions on ARP inflation-proofing (effective 2026) and maintenance of the existing CCM design. The government's UK CBAM is being legislated through the Finance Bill 2025–26 for entry into force on January 1, 2027.
coverage_notes: The UK ETS covers emissions of CO2, N2O, and PFCs in energy-intensive industries, the power sector, and aviation within the UK and European Economic Area.
relation_notes: In May 2025, the UK and EU governments announced their intention to work towards linking the UK ETS and EU ETS 1. The envisaged link would enable mutual recognition of allowances for compliance purposes, facilitate cross-system trading, and create conditions for mutual CBAM exemptions. Formal linkage has not yet been established and the UK ETS is not formally linked to any other system.

name: Uruguay CO2 tax
share_of_emissions_covered: 5% of jurisdiction emissions, 0.0042% of global emissions
sectors_covered: Agriculture, forestry and fishing fuel use
description:
In June 2021, a draft bill presented to parliament included a proposal for a carbon tax on gasoline, indicating an initial carbon rate of UYU 5,286 per tonne CO2. The draft bill intended to redefine part of the existing fuel excise charges applied to gasoline through the Specific Internal Tax (IMESI), with a tax based on carbon emissions. 
The bill became Law No. 19,996 (November 3, 2021), with Article 326 containing provisions on the carbon tax. The carbon tax (Impuesto a las Emisiones de CO2) was implemented on January 1, 2022, following the Presidential Decree 441/021, establishing a carbon tax rate of UYU 5,645.45/tCO2e that year. 
The tax rate is updated annually based on the variation of i) the Consumer Price Index and ii) the intensity of CO2 emissions per liter of gasoline. The Ministry of Industry, Energy and Mining provides this information to the Ministry of Economy and Finance and the Ministry of the Environment.
The Executive Branch is empowered to allocate a percentage  of the proceedings for financing policies that promote the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions, sustainable transportation, and climate change adaptation, with the option to establish a dedicated  fund for these purposes.
recent_developments: Decree No. 343/025: The value of the Specific Internal Tax is adjusted based on CO₂ emissions (UYU 7006 for 2026)
coverage_notes: The carbon tax covers CO2 emissions from gasoline combustion in any use.
relation_notes: not applicable

name: Vermont ETS
description:
Vermont participates in the Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative (RGGI), which covers CO₂ emissions from the state's power sector. In June 2024, the Vermont State Legislature passed the "Transportation Bill" (Act 148 of 2024), which directed the Agency of Natural Resources and the Agency of Transportation to conduct a study of a cap-and-invest program as a potential approach to meeting the state's climate emission reduction goals.

Vermont's 2025 Climate Action Plan included taking steps to join a cap-and-invest program as a priority recommendation, describing it as an overarching policy to deliver predictable GHG reductions in the transportation sector over time.
recent_developments: In February 2025, the Vermont Agency of Natural Resources and Agency of Transportation completed the cap-and-invest study mandated under Act 148 of 2024. The State Treasurer's Office, in its February 2025 report to the Legislature, recommended that Vermont not establish an independent cap-and-invest program and instead monitor the development of the New York Climate Initiative (NYCI) before pursuing participation in a regional scheme.

name: Viet Nam ETS
share_of_emissions_covered: 46% of jurisdiction emissions, 0.5044% of global emissions
gases_covered: CO2, CH4, N2O, HFCs, PFCs, and SF6
sectors_covered: Agriculture, forestry and fishing fuel use
offset_eligibility: The use of offset credits is allowed. Covered facilities may use domestic and international carbon credits for compliance alongside allowances.
description:
Vietnam's national ETS is established under the "Law on Environmental Protection" and "Decree 06/2022/ND-CP", which provide the national framework for GHG mitigation, carbon markets, and monitoring, reporting, and verification. The design and institutional arrangements for the ETS were operationalized by "Decree 119/2025/ND-CP", which entered into force in August 2025. The Ministry of Agriculture and Environment (MAE) serves as the competent authority for allowance allocation, MRV oversight, and ETS administration.

The ETS operates in a pilot phase covering 2025 to 2028, divided into two two-year periods. During the pilot, 110 facilities across three sectors are covered: 34 thermal power plants, 25 iron and steel facilities, and 51 cement producers. No numerical emissions threshold applies; facilities are covered if they appear on the approved list of major GHG-emitting facilities under Decree 06/2022 and Decree 119/2025. The system applies an intensity-based approach and allowances are allocated using sector-specific output-based benchmarks, and compliance obligations are determined ex post based on verified emissions. All allowances during the pilot phase are allocated for free.

In February 2026, Decision 263/QD-TTg approved aggregate allowance budgets for the first pilot period. The allowance budgets are 243.1 MtCO₂e for 2025 and 268.4 MtCO₂e for 2026. Installation-level allocation will be finalized by MAE based on these budgets.

From 2029, the ETS is expected to introduce auctioning, expand sectoral coverage, and pursue linkages under Article 6 cooperation.
recent_developments:
In August 2025, Decree 119/2025/ND-CP entered into force, operationalizing the Vietnam Pilot ETS design, clarifying allocation and compliance rules, and introducing flexibility provisions including banking, borrowing, and offset use. 

In January 2026, Decree 29/2026/ND-CP designated the Vietnam Stock Exchange as the authority responsible for carbon trading platform operations and strengthened provisions on market manipulation. 

In February 2026, Decision 263/QD-TTg approved aggregate GHG emission allowance budgets for the first pilot period.

The domestic carbon trading platform is expected to become operational by the end of 2026.
coverage_notes: The Vietnam Pilot ETS covers thermal power plants, iron and steel production facilities, and cement producers.
relation_notes: The Vietnam ETS is not formally linked with any other system.

name: Washington CCA
share_of_emissions_covered: 71% of jurisdiction emissions, 0.1149% of global emissions
gases_covered: CO2, CH4, N2O, SF6, HFCs, PFCs, NF3, others
sectors_covered: Agriculture, forestry and fishing fuel use
description:
Washington’s cap-and-invest program began operating in January 2023. It covers around 70% of the state’s emissions, and its cap trajectory is consistent with the long-term target to reduce statewide emissions to 95% below 1990 levels by 2050. 
 
The program covers around 100 entities in the energy, industrial, buildings, and transport sectors. Many of the cap-and-invest program’s design elements are similar to those of California’s cap-and-trade program.  Covered entities must surrender allowances for all their covered emissions. Allowances are distributed through auctioning and free allocation, with the latter based primarily on benchmarking. 
 
The cap-and-invest program was established by the “Climate Commitment Act” (CCA), signed into law by Governor Jay Inslee in May 2021. The CCA directed the Department of Ecology to pursue linkage with the cap-and-invest programs of California and Québec. Senate Bill 6058, designed to facilitate linkage, took effect in January 2025.
recent_developments:
In July 2025, the Washington Legislature enacted HB 1975 (Chapter 320, Laws of 2025), directing the Department of Ecology to analyze and model the program's allowance markets, including potential linkage scenarios. HB 1975 also established a fixed price ceiling of USD 80 per allowance for 2026 and 2027. 

Department of Ecology refiled its "Program Updates and Linkage" rulemaking (CR-101) in March 2025 and published draft rule language through spring and summer 2025, targeting proposed linkage rules in spring 2026 with a view to adoption in autumn 2026 to enable potential linkage with California and Québec.
coverage_notes: The Washington Cap and Investment program applies to GHG emissions from the industry, power, transport, waste and buildings sectors, and include industrial process emissions. The mandatory threshold is 25,000 tCO2e per year.
relation_notes: The Washington Cap-and-Invest Program is not currently linked with any other system. The "Climate Commitment Act" directed the Department of Ecology to pursue linkage with California and Québec. Senate Bill 6058, which facilitates linkage, took effect in January 2025. A joint statement was issued by California, Québec, and Washington in March 2024 on potential linkage.

name: Yucatan carbon tax
share_of_emissions_covered: 37% of jurisdiction emissions, 0.0131% of global emissions
gases_covered: CO2, CH4, N2O, HFCs, SF6, PFCs
sectors_covered: Agriculture, forestry and fishing fuel use
description:
A reform in December 2021 of the Tax Law of the State of Yucatán, added two ecological taxes including a tax on the emission of gases into the atmosphere which came into operation in 2022.

The tax on the emission of gases into the atmosphere applies to all individuals, legal entities and economic units that have fixed installations that emit the substances determined in the law (carbon dioxide, methane, nitrous oxide, hydrofluorocarbons, perfluorocarbons and sulfur hexafluoride). 

The carbon tax rate is set at 2.70 times the daily “Unit of Measurement and Update” [Unidad de Medida y Actualización (UMA)] per tonne of CO2e.

Revenues will be used to finance policies and programs for climate change, environment, health, disaster risk reduction and management, dwelling, among others.
recent_developments: No changes in the scope of the CT from 2024 to 2025 at Ley de Hacienda de Yucatán 2025
coverage_notes:
The carbon tax covers substances generated in production processes such as carbon dioxide, methane, nitrous oxide, hydrofluorocarbons, perfluorocarbons, and sulfur hexafluoride.
Individuals, legal entities, and economic units with fixed installations or sources that release emissions within the State are subject to pay the tax.
relation_notes: Not specified

name: Zacatecas carbon tax
share_of_emissions_covered: 50% of jurisdiction emissions, 0.0103% of global emissions
gases_covered: CO2, CH4, N2O, HFCs, SF6, PFCs
sectors_covered: Agriculture, forestry and fishing fuel use
description:
On January 2017, the Mexican state of Zacatecas enacted a carbon tax (impuesto de emisiones de gases a la atmósfera) as part of a broader fiscal reform which included the introduction of various ecological taxes. In 2019, the Supreme Court of Justice of the Nation validated the carbon tax.
The carbon tax rate was set at MXN 250 per tCO2e. The carbon tax applies to fixed sources and facilities. 
Entities that can prove an emissions reduction of at least 20% compared to the previous fiscal year, the tax payable in the following fiscal year will be reduced by 20%. 
Revenues are used for different purposes, mainly on environmental, sustainable development, and economic programs, and climate change mitigation and adaptation programs.
recent_developments: No changes
coverage_notes:
Zacatecas carbon tax applies to GHG emissions from fixed sources (Scope 1) from all sectors and covers all fossil fuels. It covers substances generated in production and services processes within the State.
The tax applies to any natural and legal person with fixed sources of emissions (carbon dioxide, methane, nitrous oxide, hydrofluorocarbons, perfluorocarbons, and sulfur hexafluoride).
Emissions must be directly measured or estimated. Entities may use the federal Registry on Emissions and Pollution Transfers (Registro de Emisiones y Transferencias de Contaminantes, RETC) for this.

name: Serbia carbon tax
share_of_emissions_covered: 55% of jurisdiction emissions, 0.0835% of global emissions
sectors_covered: Agriculture, forestry and fishing fuel use
description:
Serbia passed the Law on Greenhouse Gas Emissions Tax and the Law on the Tax on Imports of Carbon-Intensive Products in December 2025, both applicable from 1 January 2026. The framework introduces a national carbon tax on direct greenhouse gas emissions from covered industrial installations and a related tax on imports of carbon-intensive products, which functions as a border carbon adjustment mechanism.
The domestic carbon tax applies to emissions above predefined reference levels and is set at EUR 4 per tCO2e. Covered sectors include fertilizer and nitrogen compounds production, cement production, iron, steel and ferroalloy production, aluminum production, and electricity generation.
recent_developments: In December 2025, Serbia adopted the Law on Greenhouse Gas Emissions Tax and the Law on the Tax on Imports of Carbon-Intensive Products, establishing a national carbon pricing framework applicable from 1 January 2026.
coverage_notes:
The carbon tax covers direct (Scope 1) greenhouse gas emissions from installations operating in fertilizer and nitrogen compounds production, cement production, iron, steel and ferroalloy production, aluminum production, and electricity generation. Covered gases include CO2, N2O and PFCs.
Compliance obligations apply at the installation level to entities holding emission permits under climate regulations. Tax liability is determined based on emissions exceeding predefined reference emission levels rather than a minimum emissions threshold.
pricing_notes: The carbon tax does not include an allocation mechanism. Tax liability is calculated by subtracting predefined reference emissions from total verified emissions. Reference emissions are determined using technology-based benchmarks multiplied by the facility’s output level. This design functions as an intensity-based baseline.

name: Nigeria carbon tax
share_of_emissions_covered: 5% of jurisdiction emissions, 0.0343% of global emissions
sectors_covered: Agriculture, forestry and fishing fuel use
description: Nigeria gas flaring tax is levied on the volume of natural gas that is flared (burned off) or vented into the atmosphere without authorization. It is not a tax specifically on carbon emissions, although reducing emissions is an objective of the regulation.
recent_developments:
Nigeria’s Climate Change Act (2021) requires the government to develop a carbon tax and carbon trading system (ETS) as part of national climate policy — but a fully implemented tax has not yet started. Policy work and stakeholder engagement on a carbon tax framework is ongoing.

Nigeria finalized its Carbon Market Activation Policy in 2025, a broad framework intended to enable carbon trading, credit issuance, and emissions reporting — enabling potential revenue from carbon pricing and credits.
The government is aiming to generate climate finance revenue (up to ~$2.5–$3 billion per year) through the new carbon market framework by 2030.
coverage_notes:
The penalties cover upstream venting and flaring for oil and gas operations (Scope 1).

The Petroleum Industry Act (PIA) 2021 established a revised penalty for unauthorized gas flaring, venting, or wastage, with a penalty rate of  $3.50 per thousand standard cubic feet (Mscf) of gas flared.

name: Bolivia ETS
description: Bolivia is considering the establishment of a national ETS under a draft law titled “Regulation of the Market for Greenhouse Gas Emission Credits and Quotas of the Plurinational State of Bolivia” (PL-060/25) introduced to the parliament in November 2025.
recent_developments: As of early 2026, the Bolivian ETS remains at the legislative proposal stage. The draft law has been formally admitted into the legislative process and is under review by specialized committees.
